title: Hashing
description: Using HashMap/HashSet as a hash table to solve lookup and counting problems in O(1) average time -- delivering the faster fix promised back in Big-O Notation for hasDuplicate.
objectives: Understand hashing as O(1) average-case lookup, insert, and delete
Solve the duplicate-detection problem from Big-O Notation in O(n) using a HashSet
Solve a classic two-sum problem in O(n) using a HashMap

sections.text: In Big-O Notation, hasDuplicate used nested loops -- O(n^2) -- and we promised a faster O(n) version using a HashSet. Today we deliver on that promise, and explain WHY hashing makes it possible.
A technique that maps a value to a location (a bucket) using a hash function, enabling average O(1) lookup, insertion, and deletion -- the mechanism underlying HashMap and HashSet.

We are not implementing a hash table's internals ourselves here -- Java's HashMap and HashSet (covered in the Set and Map lessons) already provide this. This lesson focuses on RECOGNIZING when hashing solves a problem, and USING it effectively.

sections.title: Why this is O(n), not O(n^2)
points: One single loop through numbers -- no nested loop at all
seen.contains(num) and seen.add(num) are both O(1) AVERAGE case, thanks to hashing
Total work: n iterations * O(1) per iteration = O(n) overall -- dramatically faster than Big-O Notation's O(n^2) version for large arrays

sections.text: HashSet's contains() and add() are O(1) on AVERAGE, not guaranteed worst-case -- in rare pathological cases they can degrade, but this is far beyond this course's scope. In virtually all practical situations, treat them as O(1).
3. Two Sum: A Classic Hashing Problem
Given an array and a target, find two numbers that add up to it -- and this time, the array is NOT sorted, so Two Pointers' approach doesn't directly apply.

sections.title: Tracing hasPairHashing({8,3,15,6,2}, 9)
steps: num=8: complement=9-8=1. seen={} doesn't contain 1. Add 8. seen={8}
num=3: complement=9-3=6. seen={8} doesn't contain 6. Add 3. seen={8,3}
num=15: complement=9-15=-6. seen={8,3} doesn't contain -6. Add 15. seen={8,3,15}
num=6: complement=9-6=3. seen={8,3,15} DOES contain 3! Return true immediately

sections.text: complement = target - num asks: 'what value, paired with the current num, would hit target?' If we've already SEEN that complement earlier in the array, we've found our pair -- all in one single pass, no sorting required, no nested loop.
4. Counting with a HashMap: Word Frequency (Recap)

This is the exact same pattern from the Map lesson's word-count example -- HashMap's O(1) average put()/getOrDefault() is what makes counting occurrences across an entire array an O(n) operation overall, instead of something slower.
5. Putting It Together: First Non-Repeating Character

sections.title: Tracing it for "swiss"
points: First pass builds counts: s=3, w=1, i=1
Second pass walks the string IN ORDER again: 's' has count 3 (skip), 'w' has count 1 -- return 'w' immediately
Two passes, both O(n), so overall still O(n) -- much better than checking each character against every other character

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Reaching for nested loops out of habit for lookup/duplicate/pairing problems that a HashSet or HashMap could solve in a single O(n) pass.
Forgetting complement = target - num is the KEY insight for hashing-based pair-sum problems -- it turns 'find a pair' into 'have I seen this one specific value before?'
Assuming hashing requires the array to be sorted -- unlike Two Pointers, hashing works perfectly well on unsorted data.
Using counts.get(c) directly without getOrDefault() when a key might not exist yet, risking a null result where an int was expected (recall Autoboxing/Unboxing's null-unboxing pitfall).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why does using a HashSet turn duplicate-detection from O(n^2) into O(n)?
questions.answer: A single loop through the array suffices, since checking whether a value has been seen (contains()) and recording it (add()) are both O(1) on average -- eliminating the need for a nested loop to compare every pair.

questions.question: How does hashing solve the two-sum problem without sorting the array?
questions.answer: For each number, compute its complement (target - num) and check if that complement has already been seen, using a HashSet. If it has, a valid pair exists. This works in one O(n) pass regardless of the array's order, unlike Two Pointers, which requires sorted data.

questions.question: What is the average time complexity of HashMap/HashSet operations like get(), put(), contains(), and add()?
questions.answer: O(1) on average, which is what makes hashing such a powerful tool for lookup, counting, and pairing problems -- though in rare worst-case scenarios this can degrade, a detail beyond this course's scope.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Hashing maps values to locations via a hash function, giving HashMap and HashSet O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion. This turns many O(n^2) brute-force problems into O(n): checking for duplicates becomes a single pass with a HashSet tracking seen values, and two-sum becomes a single pass with a HashSet checking whether each number's complement (target - num) has already been seen. HashMap enables O(n) frequency counting via getOrDefault(), used in problems like finding the first non-repeating character. Hashing works on unsorted data, unlike Two Pointers, which requires sorted input.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Hashing = an instant lookup table -- 'have I seen this before?' answered in O(1), not by scanning. Two-sum's complement trick: don't search for the PAIR, search for whether its MATCH already walked past you.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why does a HashSet turn duplicate detection into O(n) instead of O(n^2)?
What is the 'complement' trick in hashing-based two-sum?
Does the hashing approach to two-sum require the array to be sorted?
What is the average time complexity of HashMap's get() and put()?

sections.title: Two Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. Each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.
hints: This is the exact hasPairHashing pattern from this lesson's Section 3, but returning INDICES instead of a boolean -- use a HashMap (value -> index) instead of a HashSet.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair of indices with nested loops.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A HashMap mapping each seen value to its index -- for each number, check if its complement was already seen.

sections.title: Contains Duplicate
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice, and false if every element is distinct.
hints: This is precisely this lesson's hasDuplicate function from Section 2, unchanged.

approaches.label: Brute Force
Optimal
approaches.explanation: A HashSet tracking seen values -- if a value is already present, a duplicate exists.

sections.title: Valid Anagram
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s. (Same problem as covered under Strings -- reframed here to emphasize the hashing/frequency-counting angle.)
hints: A frequency count (via a fixed-size array acting as a simple hash table, or a HashMap for general characters) is the hashing-based approach to this problem.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort both strings and compare directly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A HashMap (or fixed-size array for lowercase-only inputs) counting character frequencies -- increment for s, decrement for t, all must return to zero.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k), where k is the number of distinct characters

sections.title: Intersection of Two Arrays
sections.problemStatement: Given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, return an array of their intersection -- each element in the result must be UNIQUE, and the result can be in any order.
hints: Put one array's values into a HashSet, then check which of the other array's values are present -- add matches to a RESULT Set to automatically avoid duplicates.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every element in nums1, scan all of nums2 checking for a match, using a Set to avoid duplicate results.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Put nums1 into a HashSet, then check nums2's elements against it, collecting matches into a second Set.

sections.title: Intersection of Two Arrays II
sections.problemStatement: Given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, return an array of their intersection -- each element should appear as many times as it shows in BOTH arrays (unlike 349, duplicates in the result are allowed, up to the minimum count in each array).
hints: Instead of a HashSet, use a HashMap counting frequencies in nums1 -- for each element in nums2, include it in the result and decrement its count if the count is still positive.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each element in nums2, search nums1 for a not-yet-used match, marking it used.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A HashMap counting nums1's frequencies -- scan nums2, including and decrementing counts as matches are found.

sections.title: Isomorphic Strings
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings s and t, determine if they are isomorphic -- characters in s can be replaced to get t, with a consistent one-to-one mapping (no two characters map to the same character, and the mapping is consistent throughout).

examples.explanation: o would need to map to both o and a.

hints: Use TWO HashMaps -- one mapping s's characters to t's, another mapping t's characters back to s's -- to enforce the mapping is consistent in BOTH directions.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each character in s, check if its very first occurrence's position matches the pattern of t's corresponding character's first occurrence (comparing 'shape' via first-index arrays).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two HashMaps enforcing a consistent bidirectional mapping between s's and t's characters.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k), where k is the number of distinct characters

sections.title: Word Pattern
sections.problemStatement: Given a pattern and a string s, find if s follows the same pattern -- a bijection (consistent one-to-one mapping) between each letter in pattern and each word in s.
hints: Split s into words, then apply the exact same two-HashMap bidirectional-mapping technique as Isomorphic Strings -- just mapping characters to WHOLE words instead of characters to characters.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Split into words, then use first-occurrence-index arrays/maps to compare the 'shape' of pattern against the shape of the word sequence.
approaches.output: For pattern = "abba", s = "dog cat cat dog": true

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two HashMaps, exactly like Isomorphic Strings, but mapping characters to Strings (words) instead of characters to characters.
approaches.output: For pattern = "abba", s = "dog cat cat dog": true

sections.title: Group Anagrams
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.
hints: Two anagrams, once sorted, produce the IDENTICAL String -- use that sorted String as a HashMap key, grouping all strings that share it.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each string, compare it against every existing group's first member (via sorted comparison) to find where it belongs.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2 * k log k), n = number of strings, k = average string length

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A HashMap keyed by each string's SORTED form -- every anagram naturally maps to the same key, grouping them in one pass.

sections.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
sections.problemStatement: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest run of consecutive integers, in O(n) time.

examples.explanation: The longest consecutive run is [1,2,3,4], length 4.

hints: Put all numbers in a HashSet for O(1) existence checks.

Only START counting a sequence from a number that has NO predecessor (num - 1) in the set -- this ensures each sequence is counted exactly once, keeping the whole algorithm O(n) despite the inner while loop.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort the array, then scan for the longest run of consecutive values.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) to O(n) depending on the sort implementation

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A HashSet of all numbers; only start counting from numbers with no predecessor in the set, so each sequence is explored exactly once.
